title.value: OTC: Optimal Tool Calls via Reinforcement Learning
title.evidence: OTC: Optimal Tool Calls via Reinforcement Learning

authors.evidence: Hongru Wangα, Cheng Qianβ, Wanjun Zhongδ, Xiusi Chenβ, Jiahao Qiuσ, Shijue Huangµ, Bowen Jinβ, Mengdi Wangσ, Kam-Fai Wongα, Heng Jiβ

value: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
affiliations.evidence: αThe Chinese University of Hong Kong, βUniversity of Illinois Urbana-Champaign σPrinceton University, $^ \delta \mathsf { S u n }$ Yat-sen University, $\mu$ Hong Kong University of Science and Technology

current_issues.value: Existing approaches often overlook the efficiency and cost associated with tool usage, leading to excessive tool calls or insufficient tool use.
current_issues.evidence: While reinforcement learning (RL) has shown promise in improving TIR by optimizing final answer correctness, existing approaches often overlook the efficiency and cost associated with tool usage.

target.value: Develop a method to optimize tool usage efficiency while maintaining answer correctness.
target.evidence: We aim to develop such a method based on a foundational assumption: for each question and each model, there exists an optimal number of tool calls, defined as the minimal number required for the model to arrive at a correct answer.

framework_innovation.value: Introduced Optimal Tool Call-controlled Policy Optimization (OTC-PO) that encourages accurate answers with minimal tool calls.
framework_innovation.evidence: We propose Optimal Tool Call-controlled Policy Optimization (OTC-PO), a simple yet effective RL-based method that enables large language models (LLMs) to learn precise and adaptive control over external tool usage.

metric_innovation.value: Introduced tool productivity as a new metric to measure the effectiveness and efficiency of tool calls.
metric_innovation.evidence: We additionally define a new metric, tool productivity (TP), which measures the effectiveness and efficiency of tool calls during inference.

algorithm_innovation.value: Developed OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO algorithms that integrate with existing RL methods while optimizing tool usage.
algorithm_innovation.evidence: We instantiate this framework within both Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and Group Relative Preference Optimization (GRPO), resulting in OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO.

performance_improvement.value: Reduced tool calls by up to 73.1% and improved tool productivity by up to 229.4% without sacrificing accuracy.
performance_improvement.evidence: Experiments with Qwen-2.5 and Qwen-Math across multiple QA benchmarks show that our approach reduces tool calls by up to 73.1% and improves tool productivity by up to 229.4%, while maintaining comparable answer accuracy.

tool_call_reduction.value: OTC methods significantly reduce the number of tool calls required for correct answers.
tool_call_reduction.evidence: Our OTC achieves similar final performance but much higher tool calling efficiency comparing with the original method.

model_size_effect.value: Larger models tend to over-rely on external tools unless explicitly penalized for such behavior.
model_size_effect.evidence: As the model size increases, the TC and TP get bigger boost no matter in OTC-PPO or OTC-GRPO.

generalization_performance.value: The method maintains accuracy across different QA benchmarks while reducing tool calls.
generalization_performance.evidence: OTC-PPO outperforms the Search-R1-PPO in terms of EM on all benchmarks with Qwen2.5-7B-Base model with much less tool calls.

case_study_insights.value: OTC-PO reduces unnecessary tool calls while maximizing internal reasoning capabilities.
case_study_insights.evidence: Both OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO can reduce the number of unnecessary tool calls significantly but they achieve this in different ways.

task_definition.value: The objective is to generate the correct answer with the minimal cost of tools in the full trajectory.
task_definition.evidence: The objective of the task is to generate the correct answer with the minimal cost of tools in the full trajectory.

base_reward.value: Correctness is rewarded with a binary signal: 1 if correct, 0 otherwise.
base_reward.evidence: Most prior predominantly focuses on rule-based correctness rewards.

efficiency_reward.value: A tool-integrated reward modulates correctness with efficiency considerations.
efficiency_reward.evidence: We regard the tool-integrated reward as a coefficient of conventional reward function.

final_reward.value: The final reward combines correctness and tool efficiency.
final_reward.evidence: The final tool-integrated reward function is a product of tool efficiency and correctness.

algorithm_integration.value: OTC-PO can be integrated with existing RL algorithms like PPO and GRPO.
algorithm_integration.evidence: OTC-PO is lightweight and broadly applicable, requiring only minimal changes to standard RL pipelines.

datasets.value: NQ and HotpotQA for search; ToRL dataset for code.
datasets.evidence: We mainly follow the Search-R1 and ToRL setting and use same baselines and datasets for the fair and comprehensive evaluation.

models.evidence: Using multiple LLMs—including Qwen-2.5-3B/7B-Base and Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B/7B-Base.

parameters.value: Max turns set to 4 for search and 3 for code.
parameters.evidence: We set the max turn (maximal number of tool calls) in Search-R1 (ToRL) to 4 (3).

resources.evidence: We conduct our experiments on 8 A100-80G GPU.

search_tool_results.value: OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO outperform Search-R1 in tool call efficiency and productivity.
search_tool_results.evidence: OTC-PPO achieves significant improvement in terms of TC and TP compared with Search-R1.

code_tool_results.value: Preliminary results show similar findings in code tasks.
code_tool_results.evidence: We already observe similar findings as mentioned above and will release more results soon.

behavior_analysis.value: OTC methods balance tool usage and internal reasoning effectively.
behavior_analysis.evidence: Our method achieves same answers with baseline using fewer tool calls in mostly 80% of test cases.
